html:
XII

It was nine oвАЩclock in the morningвБ†вАФearly September.

The last of the summer trippers had gone, reluctant to leave all this beauty behind them and pledged to a prompt returnвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ With their hands filled with bulky bouquets of garden flowers, graciously bestowed by longtime servants who seemed sincerely regretful over their departure, they had edged along the slippery seats of the station bus to make room for the tardy, scurrying back from one last look at the blue bay from the terrace wall. They would go down the hill to Bellagio, cross the bay to Menaggio, and take a funny little funicular over the mountain en route westвБ†вАФand home.

The Villa Serbelloni was very quiet, this morningвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Not that it was at any time a rackety place, even when filled to its small capacity. There was something about the ineffable tranquillity of the old mansion that slowed one down to its leisurely tempo; that mellowed the voice and blurred the vision.

Its atmosphere seemed strangely sedative, giving a curious unreality to the whole region. One felt oneвАЩs self walking about through a Corot. The changing cloud-shadows on the mountains and the bay, synchronized with warming wisps of autumn breeze, played unaccountable tricks with oneвАЩs estimates of distances and hours. One never knew certainly whether it was Tuesday or ThursdayвБ†вАФor cared.

Somebody had declared it was as if the picture were blurryвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ out of focus. There were no sharp, angular outlines either to the purple hills or the turquoise lake below. The very pebbles on the carriage drive were unreal, each wrapped with a tiny, shimmering aureole of pale opalвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Each grape, in the shapely clusters dependent from the trellis sheltering the breakfast nook, was encircled by an amber nimbus as if glowing with some inner radianceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ An excellent place for daydreaming.

To appreciate it properly, however, the guest must bring to the little arbour a quiet, unworried mind, else the timeless calm of the place would only accentuate the internal tumultвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Unless one were at peace with himself, here, he could be more desperately lonely and depressed than in a desert.

The arbour was all but deserted. Except for the elderly English couple at the last table in the row against the low terraced wall, absorbed in their letters, groping occasionally for the handles of their coffee cups, Helen Hudson had the place to herself. She was so lonesome she watched with comradely concern the antics of an ambitious bee that disputed her right to the little jar of honey.

It might never be determined where was the next to the loveliest spot in the worldвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ La Jolla?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Lake Louise?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The Columbia River Highway?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Royal Gorge?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Grand Canyon?

During her three years abroad, Helen had successively shifted her allegiance from the Grand Canal under a full moon to the Upper Corniche Road, the Amalfi Drive, the Neckar glimpsed through the treetops from the crumbling balconies of Heidelberg Castle.

But there never could be any doubt about the loveliest scene in the world. She faced itвБ†вАФLake ComoвБ†вАФfrom the little arbour flanking on the east the Villa Serbelloni, on the hillcrest overlooking BellagioвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ looked at it without seeing it today; for her eyes were preoccupied.

Her morning mail had practically confirmed certain harassing suspicions. It was reasonably sure now that Monty had been manipulating her estate to her serious disadvantage. How to protect herself against grave misfortuneвБ†вАФif indeed that misfortune had not been already guaranteedвБ†вАФwithout plunging her family into disgraceful publicity, was too intricate a problem to be solved.

Not at any time since her commitment of all her affairs to Monty, upon his renewed persuasion, a year ago, had his remittances been in full of her expectations.

When, in January, he had written that Northwestern Copper was in the midst of a вАЬreorganizationвАЭ which was temporarily depressing its value and reducing its dividends, she had been disposedвБ†вАФalbeit puzzledвБ†вАФto accept his statement as correct. She made no pretence of understanding the explanation he offered with an infinitude of befuddling detail phrased in a jargon utterly incomprehensible. The situation troubled and inconvenienced her, but she had tried to believe what Monty said. There was nobody at hand to query; no one she cared or dared to consult by correspondence. She had made Monty her business agent with full power of attorney. She was in his hands to do with as he pleased. It was most disturbing.

In mid-July, he had written lengthily his deep regret and disappointment that Northwestern Copper was making so slow a recovery; still in a tangle over вАЬreorganizationвАЭ difficulties, вАЬrefinancingвАЭ problems, and вАЬthe tiresome delays of senseless litigationвАЭвБ†вАФMonty was gifted with an extraordinary capacity for redundant ambiguities. In short, Northwestern Copper had passed its semiannual period of accounting to its stockholders without declaring any dividend at allвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He was sorrier than he could sayвБ†вАФbut, of course, it wasnвАЩt his fault.

Stunned to the realization that she was alone in a foreign country without income or any assurance that it might be restored, she had spent whole days fretting about her next move in this awkward predicament.

It had occurred to her that something might be liquidated of her holdings at Brightwood. She was aware that her inherited stock in the hospital had no market value. It was not at all like ordinary commercial or industrial securities. The income was small and uncertain; the stock itself being worth just what some philanthropic purchaser might be willing to pay for it.

Moreover, there was a sentimental value attached to it in her mind. Under no circumstances, short of actual pressing need, would she have consented to part with it. But, sentiment or no sentiment, she would have to live. Her funds were very low.

After a month of worry, she had written to Nancy Ashford. Rejecting, after some debate, the thought of confiding the exact state of her affairs, she limited her inquiry to the possibility of converting her Brightwood stock into cash at the earliest moment a purchaser could be found. Mrs.¬†Ashford would be surprised and disappointed, doubtless, but she would have to think what she liked about it. The real reason could not be divulged.

вАЬNowвБ†вАФI wonder exactly why she wants to do that?вАЭ Nancy had said to Doctor Merrick, upon receipt of it. вАЬSurely she has enough to live onвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The income on her Northwestern Copper canвАЩt be a penny less than six thousand a yearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Preferred stockвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ sound as GibraltarвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you suppose somebody has been defrauding her? She knows so little about business.вАЭ

вАЬWhoвАЩs handling her affairs while she is gone?вАЭ

вАЬNobodyвБ†вАФso far as I knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ThereвАЩs nothing very complicated about her businessвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I think she said her brotherвБ†вАФher cousin, ratherвБ†вАФwould assist her in making out her income tax report.вАЭ

вАЬDвАЩyou know anything about him?вАЭ

вАЬNot a thingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Might he be handling her money?вАЭ

вАЬUnlikelyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Why should he?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Unless she gave him carte blanche to buy and sell; and she wouldnвАЩt do that.вАЭ

вАЬWhat shall I say to her, Bobby?вАЭ

вАЬHow much is her Brightwood stock worth?вАЭ

вАЬConservatively?вАЭ Nancy grinned.

вАЬNoвБ†вАФoptimistically!вБ†вАФa Micawber appraisal!вАЭ

вАЬAbout twenty thousand, I should thinkвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Want it?вАЭ

вАЬYesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You tell her you have contracted to dispose of her Brightwood stuff forвБ†вАФfor twenty-five thousand dollarsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Tell her the man wishes to pay for it in twenty-five monthly instalmentsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That will insure her against being broke, over there; and meantimeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYes?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Meantime?вАЭ

Doctor Merrick moved toward the door.

вАЬOhвБ†вАФI donвАЩt knowвБ†вАФIвАЩm sureвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs her affair.вАЭ

Again Helen took up the long letter she had just received from Mrs.¬†Ashford with its lifesaving enclosure and the definite promise of more to come. The possession of it filled her with misgivings. She had snapped an important tie connecting her with Doctor HudsonвАЩs most cherished interest. It was like closing a door on something he had lived for.

There was no reproach in the letter, either direct or impliedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Easy to detect a note of anxiety, however.

вАЬI hope this disposal of your Brightwood stock does not mean that your income from other sources is in any way depletedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Is there anything you would like to have us look after for you?вАЭ

Following the briefly clear statement of the deal she had made, and the terms of the sale, Nancy Ashford had served up a potpourri of local newsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You wouldnвАЩt know Detroit!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ New clubs, new theatres, swagger hotels, metropolitan shops!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Even the hospital was adding a new unit. Plans already drawn.

вАЬYoung Doctor Merrick is with us now. Or perhaps you knew that. Of course it is a delight to me to see the rapid progress he is making, for IвАЩve always been interested in himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Naturally he has a great advantage over the typical intern because, having known from the first day of his medical course that he was going in for brain surgery, he comes here with more specialized knowledge and experience than any other young doctor who ever joined our organizationвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Already Doctor Pyle is treating him with a deference that amuses me. (Doctor Pyle is so short with the cubs.)вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Bobby got permission to fit up a little laboratory of his own. There was a small alcoveвБ†вАФperhaps you recallвБ†вАФjust off the main solarium on the top floor. We had it partitioned. You should see the apparatus he has installed. ItвАЩs more of a physical than a chemical laboratory, I thinkвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Glass-blowing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A forge!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A blast-furnace!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ All manner of electrical things!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You canвАЩt get any information out of himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I was in there yesterday, and asked him if he was making a new radio set, and he said, вАШSomething like that.вАЩвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But that doesnвАЩt mean much when he says it, except that he wishes you wouldnвАЩt bother him.

вАЬOhвБ†вАФI never had a bigger thrill in my life than at his graduation! I had hoped so much for him, all alongвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And when it came to the big dayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ with his name starred on the Commencement programme (he hadnвАЩt said a word to me about his taking second honours!) well, I just sat there and wept silly tearsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ His mother couldnвАЩt come, so I pretended I was his motherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And you should have seen his grandfather! Proud? When the class marched across the platform for their diplomas, dear old Mr.¬†Merrick stood in his seat, trying to wave his handkerchief and blow with it at the same time, until somebody pulled him down by the coattails.вАЭ

The sheets trembled in HelenвАЩs hands as she read.

The last line on that pageвБ†вАФit was numbered eightвБ†вАФhad been scratched out, but was still legible:

вАЬThe young doctor who receivedвБ†вАФвАЭ

The next page had been originally numbered twelve. That had also been ineffectually deleted in favour of nine.

On second thought, Nancy Ashford had omitted a considerable part of her letter.

вАЬHereвАЩs that draft for a thousand,вАЭ Bobby Merrick had said, as he stood by NancyвАЩs desk.

вАЬThanks. IвАЩve just finished writing to her. IвАЩll get it off today.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs good!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ By the wayвБ†вАФyou said nothing about the Dawsons, did you?вАЭ

вАЬWhyвБ†вАФyesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Rather IвАЩd not?вАЭ

вАЬLeave it outвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I donвАЩt think sheвАЩd be interested muchвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ andвБ†вАФwell, I have a reason.вАЭ

вАЬAs you like,вАЭ said Nancy, hastily extracting the pages on which she had written:

вАЬвБ†вАФfirst honours is Doctor MerrickвАЩs closest friendвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Damon and PythiasвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the kind of a friendship you read about! They worked together throughout their medical courseвБ†вАФsame speciality. First honours now carry a prize offered by Mr.¬†Owen Simmons (Simmons Turbine Co.)вБ†вАФa year in Vienna, all expenses, scholarship, liberal allowance, etcвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The Dawsons were here last ThursdayвБ†вАФthe day your letter came. She was going to New York to see her husband off.

вАЬActually, the three of them were like brothers and sister. I couldnвАЩt help being jealous. They were closeted here in my office for a whole hour, and when they came out, and I met them in the hall, Mrs.¬†Dawson was all excited. She seemed so happy that I said, вАШYou look like Christmas morning!вАЩ вАШWhy shouldnвАЩt I?вАЩ she replied, laughing. вАШIвАЩm going along!вАЩвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЩTo Europe?вАЩвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЩAbsolutely!вАЩ

вАЬThey have a dear little boyвБ†вАФgoing on four. I never saw a child so beautifully trained. Doctor Merrick went along with them to New York and brought little Jack back with him to stay at Windymere until Mrs.¬†Dawson returns. I dare say old Mr.¬†Merrick can be found, at this minute, leading the pony he has bought for him.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

There was a letter from Joyce:

вАЬNo, darling, IвАЩm sure you will take no pleasure in thinking or saying вАШI told you so.вАЩ For that wouldnвАЩt be youвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Never a day passes that I do not scourge myself for the abominable way I used you that winter when you were trying so hard to step in between me and myself.

вАЬI wasnвАЩt fair to anybody through those days. Not even to poor Bobby. I never told you the straight of that. I meant to; but, you see, I telephoned to him the next morning and asked him to come and see me. IвАЩd a hazy notion that he wasвБ†вАФwellвБ†вАФkind to me the night before, and I thought he might renew our friendship. He made some excuse. I was hurt and humiliated. I knew something had happened to cause an estrangement between you and Bobby, forвБ†вАФcan you forgive me, darling?вБ†вАФI noticed a letter you had from him shortly afterward, and peeked into it. He wanted to be forgiven for causing you a lot of suffering. I knew that meant he hadnвАЩt told you all the truth. You thought he was in that souse party at GordonвАЩs, and I just let you think soвБ†вАФafter he refused to see me again. The honest fact was that he went there sober and fought everybody in the place to get me out when I was so blind drunk I didnвАЩt really learn what it was all about for three days. I know Bobby means nothing to you and that you despise him, but itвАЩs only fair to him that you should know how he happened to bring me home, that Thanksgiving night.

вАЬNo, darling, itвАЩs not any better. ItвАЩs never going to be any better. I know that now. Tommy canвАЩt help it. One night he comes home drunk, surly and obstinate; the next night drunk, silly and sentimental; the next night drunk, argumentative and critical; the next night drunk, savage and abusiveвБ†вАФbut always drunk. I can definitely count on that! I never know what mood he will be inвБ†вАФwhether I am to be upbraided for imagined indifference to him and his work (little enough he does of it!) or pestered with pretences of an affection neither of us feelsвБ†вАФbut I can always be sure of one thing: he will be drunk!

вАЬAt first, he claimed he wrote better when stimulated, and I believed him; drank with himвБ†вАФall times of the day, in all sorts of places, with all kinds of people. He said it helped him to the local colour necessary to his story writing. I took his word for it. Then I saw that it was rapidly doing him up. He was losing his magazine market. His things began to come back with curt little notes. I hated to nag him, but the time came when I had to rebel against being dragged about with his greasy crowd of drunken pretenders to some sort of literary or artistic talentвБ†вАФwould-beвАЩs and has-beens!

вАЬNow he is out on his own, doing little or nothing. Thank God, we donвАЩt have to worry about the rent or where the next meal is coming from. Dear old dad saw to that. So long as they continue to mine copper in the Upper Peninsula, Tommy and I can go through the motions of living, but itвАЩs a dull business and life has gone flat. I would leave him tomorrow if it were not that I feel under a sort of obligation. IвАЩm as much responsible for his habits as anyone. What would you do in my place?вАЭ

SoвБ†вАФnothing whatever ailed Northwestern Copper or Joyce would be having trouble with her dividends. She would write to Monty and press some serious questions. Had Monty volunteered to look after her business with the purpose of appropriating her income? Why had she not sought counsel before putting herself at his mercy? There were plenty of reliable trust companiesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Perhaps there was an explanationвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, he should have his chance to offer itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ very soon, too! She would write today!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ NoвБ†вАФtomorrow. She wasnвАЩt up to it todayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ So he was taking Brightwood by storm, was he?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Glass-blowing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Whatever for?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What had surgery to do with glass-blowing?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Why hadnвАЩt he told the truth about that episode at GordonвАЩs!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She was glad his letter of apology had been too vague for Joyce to understandвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Poor Joyce!

Gathering up her mail, she arose from the table, smiled at old Martino as he drew back her chair, and strolled slowly down the winding carriage-drive. The gay little parasol refused to share her trouble, and brightened her face. She descended the narrow flight of steps to the next level of the mountain road that spiralled up from the village; followed a graceful arc of it to the second long flight which widened to a street flanked by picturesque little shops.

Yesterday she had promised herself that this morning she would make an excursion to the Villa Carlotta. Diagonally across the bay, on a heavily wooded shore of indescribable beauty, the famous home of an absentee prince was open to touristsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Some important Canovas, some rare orchids, a wide variety of exoticsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She must see them. Everyone did.

There was no lack of attentive service as she stepped down into the red-and-blue canopied motorboatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It required many assistants at the little wharf in front of the Villa Carlotta to attend to her disembarkation. The diminutive parasol was handed up to the nearest member of the reception committee, the Baedeker in its leather cover was passed to another, the field-glasses on a strap were unslung from her shoulder; and, regretting with a gesture of dismay that there was nothing further to take off, she reached up both hands and permitted the envied pair who remained uncommissioned to help her over the gunwale.

Smilingly collecting her possessions, she ascended to the huge iron gates and into the coolness of the great hallвБ†вАФceiled, walled, floored with white marble.

A young American woman, about her own ageвБ†вАФpossibly a little olderвБ†вАФwas seated on a graceful marble benchвБ†вАФthe only place in the room where one might sitвБ†вАФintent upon the famous Cupid and Psyche. She was modish in a grey tailored suit with a close fitting grey hat fringed with tawny curls.

With a brief glance they took each otherвАЩs measure, nodded, smiled. Helen sat down beside her.

вАЬItвАЩs the best thing he did, donвАЩt you think?вАЭ ventured the girl in grey.

вАЬExquisite!вАЭ

WellвБ†вАФshe ought to know what things were exquisite, reflected Marion. The word described herselfвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Somewhere in this vicinity she expected to meet a young woman with blue eyes, long lashes, blue-black hair probably coiffed in what was known as a windblown bob, a smile as tantalizing as Mona LisaвАЩs, and a voice that made you think of a cello. (вАЬBobbyвБ†вАФfor HeavenвАЩs sake!вАЭ she had protested. вАЬAre you sure it isnвАЩt like a heavenly harp?вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬWellвБ†вАФsomething like that,вАЭ he had agreed.)

вАЬWhat else is there to see?вАЭ she inquired, after a long silence.

вАЬI never was here before,вАЭ Helen replied. вАЬThe gardens, I think, and some foreign trees and ferns. Shall we look? YouвАЩre alone, arenвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬQuiteвБ†вАФalone and lonesome.вАЭ

The patter of their heels echoed through the spacious corridor as they sought the autumn sunshine. On the terrace they hesitated, inquired of an attendant, and took the broad path northward through the artfully landscaped gardens.

вАЬYou came over from Bellagio?вАЭ

She had. Last night she had arrived from Lugano, and was stopping at a little hotel in the villageвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Thought sheвАЩd stay a week, perhaps.

вАЬOhвБ†вАФthen you must move up to the Villa Serbelloni. IвАЩd be so happy if you did!вАЭ

Immediately they had disclosed their identities, the budding friendship developed with all the rapidity natural to a meeting of two lonely fellow-countrymen in a foreign land. Young Mrs.¬†DawsonвАЩs story was quickly told.

вАЬHeвАЩll be all the better for not having me to bother with until he settles into his routine,вАЭ she explained. вАЬAnd, anyway, itвАЩs my first experience of Europe. I want to ramble about and see things.вАЭ

вАЬQueer you should have come to Bellagio direct from Paris. IвАЩm glad you did, of course; but thereвАЩs nothing here but an amazingly fine viewвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ People donвАЩt, you know. They go toвБ†вАФoh, down into the ch√Ґteau country, or along the Riviera; Rome, Naples, FlorenceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How did you happen to come here?вАЭ

вАЬI read about it in a bookвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ long time agoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Always wanted to come here!вАЭ

It was fun to search for mutual interests. Doctor Dawson had just finished the Medical School in June. His first honours had taken him to Vienna. Brain surgeryвБ†вАФthat was his specialityвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Helen had had a letter, just this morning, from a friend in Detroit who knew intimately the young doctor taking second honours in that class. Without doubt, Doctor Dawson knew him well.

вАЬMerrick?вАЭ MarionвАЩs brows wrinkled in an attempt at remembrance. вАЬOh, yesвБ†вАФtall, serious chap, wasnвАЩt he? But, you never met himвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve seen himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I think that describes himвБ†вАФpretty well.вАЭ

вАЬWhat a duck of a grotto!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ LetвАЩs go down!вАЭ

They descended into the mossy, fern-fringed enclosure and rested on the circular stone seat, facing a stucco Pan on a graceful pedestal.

вАЬWhat did he have to be so serious about?вАЭ queried Helen. вАЬSurely things should have come easy enough for him!вАЭ

вАЬDo you think he looks serious?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ WhyвБ†вАФheвАЩs the most roguish thing I ever saw!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Serious?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ With that impish grin?вАЭ

вАЬOhвБ†вАФyou mean Pan!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HeвАЩs a little devil!вАЭ

вАЬAnd you were still thinking about young Doctor Merrick.вАЭ Marion pinioned her lower lip in an understanding smile and mysteriously half closed one eye. вАЬMaybe he wasnвАЩt serious, at all. He wouldnвАЩt need to be. Awfully rich, isnвАЩt he?вАЭ

They wandered on, occasionally coming upon delicious surprisesвБ†вАФa short flight of worn steps by a wall mantled with Banksian roses descending to a shady water-gateвБ†вАФa little classic pavilion, the flagging strewn with fugitive yellow leaves. Marion loosed her imagination, prattling of romances and intrigues sheltered by these sequestered nooks, through the years.

вАЬHeвАЩs at Brightwood now,вАЭ observed Helen, at the first full stop in her new friendвАЩs rhapsody. вАЬThat was Doctor HudsonвАЩs hospital; soвБ†вАФnaturallyвБ†вАФIвАЩm interested.вАЭ

вАЬYesвБ†вАФyou would be.вАЭ Marion smiled, cryptically.

It was far past noon now. The little boat that had brought Helen over was moored at the wharf. They were helped into it. Neither spoke for full five minutesвБ†вАФHelen regarding their silver trail on the placid water, MarionвАЩs eyes held by the lovely terraces and gates of the Villa.

вАЬI think,вАЭ said Marion dreamily, вАЬthat is the most wonderfully beautiful place I ever saw!вАЭ

вАЬWhat I canвАЩt understandвАЭвБ†вАФHelenвАЩs face was a study in perplexityвБ†вАФвАЬis how they could have helped knowing each otherвБ†вАФintimatelyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Taking the honours togetherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and specializing in the same thingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ a very restricted field, too!вАЭ

Marion turned and regarded her with a slow smile.

вАЬIf IвАЩd known I was ever to find somebody who was so interested in him, IвАЩd have made it my business to get acquaintedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ LetвАЩs poke about in some of those funny little shops before we go upвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Want to?вАЭ

Marion Dawson went to her room that nightвБ†вАФshe had moved up to the VillaвБ†вАФwith many troublesome misgivings. So far as the success of her mission was concerned, it was assured. Bobby had sent her to find out the whole truth about HelenвАЩs financial misfortunes and to put him on the track of their possible remedy. He had confided that his interest was in the main philanthropic. Had Doctor Hudson lived, she would not have met this disaster; and Doctor HudsonвАЩs death was more or less chargeable to him. At least, he admitted a heavy responsibility. Her welfare was his concern.

вАЬSure there isnвАЩt any more to it than that, Bobby?вАЭ she had teased.

вАЬI wish there was more to it,вАЭ he had confessed, вАЬbut there canвАЩt beвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩve quite put that idea out of my mindвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ FranklyвБ†вАФshe hates the sight of me!вАЭ

It hadnвАЩt required much feminine intuition to discover that BobbyвАЩs estimate of Helen HudsonвАЩs attitude toward him was exactly wrongвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How she could delight him, if she wished, with an impressionistic report of todayвАЩs conversationвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But that wouldnвАЩt be fairвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Precisely where was her allegiance in this matter?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was traitorous enough to extract HelenвАЩs confidence about her money difficultiesвБ†вАФbut that would be ultimately to her advantage. When she learnedвБ†вАФif she ever didвБ†вАФhow her financial anxieties were relieved, she would not question the methodвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But she would never forgive a breach of confidence about her interest in BobbyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Really, it was most unpleasantвБ†вАФbeing a spy.

All afternoon they had been together, rambling in and out of the crooked little streets; at four, grinding laboriously up the hill in the ancient fiacre with the high, steel-tired wheels; at seven, tarrying over their dinner in the arbourвБ†вАФeach conscious of a friendship destined, they felt, to become very valuable. Helen had insisted upon her having a room next her own, on the south side where the big balconied windows looked out upon the bayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Tomorrow they were having an early breakfast so they could catch the little steamer on its first trip of the day to Como.

вАЬIвАЩd flick this job tonight,вАЭ wrote Marion, вАЬand come straight home, if it wasnвАЩt that I knew my detective work would benefit her. SheвАЩs been terribly lonely, awfully troubled; and sheвАЩs going to tell me all about it in the next few days. I wonвАЩt have to ask her a single question. SheвАЩs going to tell me of her own accord. But I do feel so mean, Bobby, with this deception. What an adorable creature she is! I never met anybody to whom I was so quickly drawn. Please donвАЩt ever let her find out about my part in this. I donвАЩt believe I could bear it if she learned I had cultivated her for a purpose!вАЭ

The shopkeepers in little Bellagio became quite accustomed to the sight of two remarkably attractive young American women on their streets, and the skippers of the pleasure craft, plying Lake Como, were proud to have them for frequent passengers. Every morning they breakfasted together in the arbour, every evening they strolled, arm in arm, through the lovingly tended gardens of the hotel.

There was very little about each otherвАЩs story that they did not know now. Their confidences had been tender, girlish, unreserved. It was no ordinary friendship. From the first moment, they had been irresistibly attracted, and made no effort to sustain the reticence each would have felt, naturally, toward a stranger.

All forenoon of that tragic Tuesday, which they were to remember with agony, they had hiked along a tortuous mountain road above Menaggio. Helen had laid bare her whole dilemma in the case of her business dealings with Monty, and was strongly counselled to wait a while and do nothing until her return to the States, seeing that her income was assured for the presentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ With the bars all down, she talked freely about Bobby too, confessing by her tone all that she hesitated to put into words.

It began to rain after luncheon. They agreed upon a siesta, and went to their roomsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ An hour later, Helen, having wakened, decided to write some letters. She remembered she had left her guidebook in MarionвАЩs room. Quietly turning the knob and finding the door unlocked, she tiptoed across the room, smiling at the sleeping face on the pillow, and took up her Baedeker from the writing-desk. Beside it, stamped and ready for mailing, was a bulky letter addressed to Dr.¬†Robert Merrick, Brightwood Hospital, Detroit, Michigan, U.S.A.

She was stunnedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ as if someone had dealt her a blow over the heart. Scarcely able to breathe, she groped her way blindly out of the room, so fearful of rousing Marion that she left the door ajar rather than risk rattling the latch. For a long time she sat on the edge of her bed, shoulders bowed, hands listless on her knees. The world had caved in. With hot cheeks, she recalled some of the things she had whispered to Marion DawsonвБ†вАФconfidences no Inquisition machinery could have twisted out of herвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Doubtless all these impulsive confessions had been spread on paper to satisfy the curiosity of Bobby Merrick. It was clear enough now!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What an odd coincidence they had thought itвБ†вАФshe and MarionвБ†вАФthat they had been brought together in this accidental way, and found the dearest friendship either of them had ever known!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ CoincidenceвБ†вАФindeed!

Marion slept heavily until five and roused with the uneasy sensation that something unpleasant had occurred. It was raining torrents. The room was dark. A strong draught was blowing through. The door was open. She distinctly remembered having closed it.

Suddenly she gasped and clutched at her throat with both hands. She walked with short, reluctant steps to the desk. HelenвАЩs Baedeker was gone! It would have been impossible for her to recover it without noticing the letter. She flung herself across the bed, swept with remorse.

A half hour later, frantically drumming on her temples with her fingers, she resolved to take the letter to Helen and beg her to read it. She would tell the whole story, and try to explain how she came to be involved in this benevolent treachery.

Heart pounding, face flushed, she tapped gently at HelenвАЩs door and received no response; tried the latch and found it locked.

Returning to her room, she nervously dressed for dinner, and went slowly down the winding staircase; searched the lounge, glanced into the dining-room; finally summoned her courage to approach the desk of the concierge.

вАЬHas Mrs.¬†Hudson come down?вАЭ she asked, with a dry throat.

вАЬShe is gone, madameвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You did not know?вАЭ

вАЬGone?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You mean she has left the hotel?вАЭ

вАЬAbout four oвАЩclock, madame.вАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФwhere?вАЭ

вАЬShe left no address, madameвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She said she would send for her trunks, later.вАЭ

Marion turned slowly away and retraced her steps spiritlessly to the foot of the stairs; then, after some hesitation, came again to the desk.

вАЬWill you see if there is a message for me?вАЭ

Obediently he went through the motions of inspecting several pigeonholes on the wall behind him and thumbed a pack of letters, looking for something neither of them expected he would find.

It poured hard all night, and the Villa SerbelloniвБ†вАФif not the loneliest place in the worldвБ†вАФwas a close runner-up to the Continental Hotel in Milan, for that distinction.
